title: Sage tea for hot flushes
subtitle: A mildly oestrogenic infusion for perimenopausal hot flushes
excerpt: A simple infusion of dried sage leaf. Traditionally used for perimenopausal hot flushes and excessive sweating. Sage has mild oestrogenic activity; the infusion is taken cold or at room temperature for best effect.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, sage entry: the astringent, antiperspirant, and oestrogenic tradition, the traditional use for menopausal sweating. the herbal reference on Salviae officinalis folium, the well-established use for excessive sweating, and the traditional use for menopausal symptoms.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare in the morning and chill. Drunk cold or at room temperature throughout the day is the traditional perimenopausal regimen. Dried sage stores 12 months in a sealed tin.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plant compound with mild oestrogenic activity. Sage contains phytoestrogenic compounds that may explain its traditional use for perimenopausal hot flushes. The activity is much weaker than pharmaceutical HRT.

content.text: Sage (Salvia officinalis) has a long place in European herbal medicine for hot flushes and night sweats. Maud Grieve, the early 20th-century botanical writer, records it as a remedy for menopausal sweating. The

and sweat-reducing effects (tannins reduce sweating) account for the traditional use. The infusion works best drunk cold or at room temperature through the day. Drinking it hot is less helpful for hot flushes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy. Do not use sage at therapeutic dose in pregnancy. Sage has documented uterine-stimulating and uterotonic properties at therapeutic doses. Culinary sage in cooking is safe; a daily cup of sage tea is outside this envelope.

Breastfeeding. Sage tea is the traditional milk-suppressing herb: it reduces milk production. Do not use during established breastfeeding unless the specific aim is weaning.

Thujone content. The aromatic oil of sage contains thujone at standard culinary and infusion doses. Long-term daily use at very high doses could theoretically raise thujone exposure; standard therapeutic doses (2-4 g dried leaf, 2-3 cups per day) are within the safe range at standard doses.

Hormone-sensitive conditions. Sage's mild oestrogenic activity is a consideration for readers with oestrogen-sensitive cancers or conditions. Consult your oncologist or gynaecologist before use.

Hot flushes in younger readers. Hot flushes that occur in a reader not approaching menopause, or that are accompanied by unexplained weight change, night sweats, heart palpitations, or other symptoms, should be assessed by a doctor.

items.prepNote: dried Salvia officinalis leaf, about 1 teaspoon; not ornamental sage or clary sage
just off the boil; cool the infusion before drinking for best effect

content.text: Place the dried sage in a covered teapot. Pour over the just-boiled water. Put the lid on and steep for 10 minutes. Strain into a glass. Allow to cool to room temperature, or chill in the fridge for 15-20 minutes. Drink cold or at room temperature. For hot-flush management, the traditional approach is to prepare 2-3 cups' worth in the morning (using 4-6 g sage in 600-750 ml water) and drink it throughout the day as a cold infusion.

Two to three cups per day (each containing 2 g dried sage), drunk cold or at room temperature. Take for up to 4-8 weeks continuously. If hot flushes do not improve, or if they are severe and significantly affecting sleep and daily life, speak to your GP about all options including HRT.
The prepared infusion keeps 24 hours in the fridge in a sealed jar. Dried sage stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy

In pregnancy. During active breastfeeding. With oestrogen-sensitive cancer or conditions without specialist guidance. Hot flushes with unexplained weight change, night sweats, or palpitations need medical evaluation.
